chunks: Compiler Backend Emulator DEEPAK.B KISHOR.R GOWTHAM.A Department of CSE-AI/ML SRM Institute of Science and Technology ABSTRACT Department of CSE-AI/ML SRM Institute of Science and Technology Department of CSE-AI/ML SRM Institute of Science and Technology The Compiler Backend Emulator is a system responsible for emulating and analyzing the behavior of compiler backend components that relate to code generation, optimization, and target machine simulation. A compiler backend is an integral part of a compiler; it takes intermediate code in and transforms it into efficient machine- level instructio

a compiler; it takes intermediate code in and transforms it into efficient machine- level instructions while ensuring resource utilization and optimal performance. Such an emulator provides a unique educational and experimental environment for studying back-end processes-instruction selection, register allocation, and code optimization-without actual hardware. This is achieved through the emulation of the target architecture so that developers and students can trace and assess how high-level code is being transformed into low-level executable form. As a project, this contributes to a better un

e is being transformed into low-level executable form. As a project, this contributes to a better understanding of compiler design principles, makes the testing of optimization techniques possible, and develops into a useful tool for research and learning in compiler construction and computer architecture. I. INTRODUCTION The compiler backend is part of a compiler system that takes the IR to machine code for a particular target. It performs tasks such as instruction selection, scheduling, register allocation, and code emission. Compiler backend emulators, or emulators of compiler backends, sim

er allocation, and code emission. Compiler backend emulators, or emulators of compiler backends, simulate and study the behavior of virtual machines created by compiler backends. They can investigate instruction generation, model performance, and analyze portability without the need for hardware. The authors introduce the design, implementation, and evaluation of a backend emulator in this paper. They show how it can support rapid experimentation, visualize backend behavior, offer comparative metrics across architectures, and enhance usability and security when code generation is performed. In

trics across architectures, and enhance usability and security when code generation is performed. In this paper, we present: A review of some selected literature contributions on compiler backend generation methods and emulation techniques. Visualization techniques for CPU and backend behaviour, including but not restricted to instruction streams, pipelines, and scheduling. A comparison of metrics between real and emulated backends, including code size, runtime performance, and compilation time. Identification of Research Gaps. A Gantt-chart style visualization of the emulator's implementation

Identification of Research Gaps. A Gantt-chart style visualization of the emulator's implementation and evaluation. Details on performance metrics calculation, security, and usability features along with results, discussions, and conclusions. It involves a number of steps to compile a high-level programming language into machine-understandable code, which are the frontend, intermediate representation, and backend. Among these, the compiler backend has a vital role in translating IR to optimized machine instructions targeted to any particular machine architecture. It is responsible for core ac

machine instructions targeted to any particular machine architecture. It is responsible for core activities that include instruction selection, register allocation, scheduling of instructions, and code emission. However, hardware complexity, limited observability, and a lack of sophisticated visualization tools make it increasingly difficult to analyze, test, and debug compiler backends on real hardware. This creates a growing demand for a compiler backend emulator: a software simulation environment that emulates the backends’ code generation, optimization, and scheduling processes. A compile

nment that emulates the backends’ code generation, optimization, and scheduling processes. A compiler backend emulator enables researchers, students, and compiler developers to: Model and simulate backend behavior without the need for target hardware. Visualize internal processes such as instruction flow, register allocation, and scheduling. Compare performance metrics such as execution time, instruction throughput, or code size across compiler backends or optimization levels. Improve debugging and optimization through detection of pipeline hazards and inefficient code sequences. Ensure securi

and optimization through detection of pipeline hazards and inefficient code sequences. Ensure security and correctness by validating the code generation logic and detecting possible vulnerabilities in the backend translation process. With heterogeneous architecture, AI accelerators, and custom ISAs becoming more common, the modern computing landscape urgently needs retargetable and analyzable compiler backends. Backend emulation provides a safe, efficient platform for designing, testing, and optimizing backends for such architectures before deploying them into real environments. This research

imizing backends for such architectures before deploying them into real environments. This research work represents a systematic study and implementation of a compiler backend emulator; it focuses on design methodology, CPU visualization, comparative evaluation, and security features.

II.LITERATURE REVIEW There is literature on automatic/retargetable compiler backend generation. For example, Compiler Backend Generation for Application Specific Instruction Set Processors by Cao et al. (2011) describes the use of an Architecture Description Language (ADL) and rule-library to retarget the backend of an industrial-strength compiler for ASIPS. Another recent contribution, Generation of Compiler Backends from Formal Models of Hardware by Smith, 2024, targets the issues of the automatic generation of compiler back-ends directly from formal models of hardware, with improvements in

atic generation of compiler back-ends directly from formal models of hardware, with improvements in correctness and optimization potential. Similarly, a more recent system - ACT: Automatically Generating Compiler Backends from Tensor Accelerator ISA Descriptions (2025), focuses on tensor-accelerator ISAs with the goal of fully automatic generation. Beyond backend generation, there This is work on instruction selection, for example, a survey by Blondell, which addresses one of the central backend problems. However, fewer works address the concept of emulating the compiler backend, that is, runn

problems. However, fewer works address the concept of emulating the compiler backend, that is, running the decoding, scheduling, code- generation phases in an emulator or simulation environment, for the purpose of visualization, comparative metrics, security/usability studies, and performance modelling. This lacuna forms the motivation for our research. Key themes from the literature: Retargetable compiler backends:  Automatic generation, ADL description languages, rule libraries.  Formal modeling of hardware to aid in backend generation and correctness.  Instruction selection, scheduling,

eling of hardware to aid in backend generation and correctness.  Instruction selection, scheduling, register allocation optimization techniques.  Emulation/simulation is used in computer architecture to a lesser degree for compiler backends. While there is rich literature on compiler backend generation, the domain of backend emulation itself for analysis, visualization, usability, and security remains under-explored. III.VISUALIZATION IN CPU SCHEDULING Visualization in the CPU context involves presenting, in visual and interactive ways, the internal operations that happen within a processor, like the flow of instructions, pipeline stages, hazard occurrences, and register allocation. A compiler backend emulator enables developers and researchers to visualize and observe how the generated instructions would work with the processor pipeline and resources. 1. Instruction Flow Visualization The emulator transforms these compiler IRs into low-level instructions. A visualization layer adds a

he emulator transforms these compiler IRs into low-level instructions. A visualization layer adds a step-by-step mapping to these transformations by showing:  Instruction generation and scheduling order.  Mapping of virtual registers to physical registers.  Dependencies between consecutive instructions. MAC results became an important instrument in world politics, helping to establish mutual understanding among the states of the region. This helps in understanding the effectiveness of instruction scheduling, the presence of stalls, and optimization opportunities. 2. Pipeline Visualization A

tion scheduling, the presence of stalls, and optimization opportunities. 2. Pipeline Visualization A CPU pipeline can be thought of as a series of stages, usually Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, and Writeback, where several instructions are in different phases of execution. Timeline charts in the backend emulator visualize each instruction's journey through the pipeline; this can be used to find: Pipeline hazards: data, control, and structural. Instruction latency and throughput. Efficiency of parallelism between instructions. Below is a typical example of pipeline visualization. 3. Register A

arallelism between instructions. Below is a typical example of pipeline visualization. 3. Register Allocation and Live Range Visualization Register allocation is a vital backend process. Visualization aids by highlighting:  The live range of variables.  Assignment registration per function block.  Points of register spill and reload operations. These visual tools allow the backend designer to optimize register pressure and reduce memory accesses to improve the performance of the compiled code. 4. Instruction Scheduling and Hazard Detection
. 4. Instruction Scheduling and Hazard Detection

Visualization Gantt-style or timeline-based charts show when each instruction starts execution and how pipeline hazards or dependencies delay subsequent instructions. This makes it very intuitive to identify where reordering or optimization could give better CPU utilization. 5. Comparative Visualization The backend emulator is able to render side-by-side visual comparisons between different backend targets, or optimization levels, such as 01, 02, 03. This comparative view assists in quickly highlighting performance trade-offs across architectures or compiler strategies. IV. COMPARATIVE STUDIES

lighting performance trade-offs across architectures or compiler strategies. IV. COMPARATIVE STUDIES AND METRICS We define a set of metrics to be used to evaluate the backend emulator and run comparative studies comparing real versus emulated behavior. Metrics may include: Code size in bytes of the generated binaries/assembly from real backend vs emulator Execution/runtime performance of generated code, on real hard ware if possible, against performance estimate from emulator Compilation time-time to generate code for real backend vs. emulator Backend throughput: e.g., number of instructions s

me to generate code for real backend vs. emulator Backend throughput: e.g., number of instructions selected per second, scheduling throughput Resource usage: register spill count, instruction count, instruction bundle utilization, pipeline stalls/hazards. Emulation accuracy: Error or deviation between the emulator's predicted performance versus real hardware performance, for example. Usability metrics: time taken by developers to inspect/understand back-end behaviour using the visualization tools; number of bugs/insights found. The idea of this comparative study is to select a few target archi

s; number of bugs/insights found. The idea of this comparative study is to select a few target architectures/backends-say, x86 or embedded ASIPs-and fix one benchmark suite-say, MI Bench-to compare metrics from a real backend versus our emulator. There are previous works, such as the Brandner thesis, showing comparisons of automatically generated backends versus GCC backends for execution time and code size. V. RESEARCH GAP From the literature review and comparison studies, we identify the following research gaps: 1. Specialized emulators for compiler backends that integrate visualization, per

wing research gaps: 1. Specialized emulators for compiler backends that integrate visualization, performance modelling, and usability features are lacking. 2. Very limited work in correlating the emulator-predicted metrics (e.g., Scheduling Stalls, pipeline hazards) with real hardware Runtime/Performance across different targets; 3. Lack of consideration of security/usability in code generation workflows in backends: e.g., how to detect insecure generated code, vulnerabilities in register - allocation or unpredictable backend behaviour. 4. Few tools support developer-centric visualization of t

cation or unpredictable backend behaviour. 4. Few tools support developer-centric visualization of the backend internals, like instruction selection decisions and register live-range evolution within an emulator environment. 5. The need for a unified methodology, including Gantt-chart style scheduling and visualization, comparative metrics, so as to enable research into backend emulation rather than isolated case studies. This motivates our proposed work: to build a backend-emulator framework with rich visualization, comparative metrics, and evaluation of security/usability aspects and validat

th rich visualization, comparative metrics, and evaluation of security/usability aspects and validate it through case studies. VI. SYSTEM DESIGN AND METHODOLOGY CBES is designed as a modular, scalable, architecture- independent framework that emulates the core operations of a compiler backend within a simulated environment. Developers and researchers can use it to analyze, visualize, and evaluate performance of the backend without actual hardware. The following is what the emulator architecture focuses on: 1.Emulation of backend processes such as instruction selection, register allocation, and

focuses on: 1.Emulation of backend processes such as instruction selection, register allocation, and instruction scheduling 2. Internal compiler operations visualized and analyzed for better explainability. 3. Performance and security evaluation, using quantitative metrics and structured methodologies. System Architecture

The CBES framework consists of the following interrelated modules: 1. Frontend Input Interface : Accepts IR/AST from compilers such as LLVM or GCC. Translates high-level IR to a structured form that the emulator core can support. 2. Backend Emulator Core : Emulates the important back-end phases: selection of instructions, allocation of registers, and scheduling. Emulates code emission through the generation of pseudo-assembly or machine instructions. 3. Instruction and Pipeline Simulator: Models the CPU pipeline behavior, including fetch, decode, execute, memory, and writeback stages. It inclu

the CPU pipeline behavior, including fetch, decode, execute, memory, and writeback stages. It includes data, control, and structural hazard detection, along with cycle-by-cycle visualization. 4. Metrics Collector and Analyzer: Measures a variety of performance parameters related to compilation time, instruction count, code size, and estimated runtime. Stores performance data in a database for comparison analysis and visualization. 5. Visualization Dashboard Displays the CPU pipeline flow, instruction timing charts, register allocation graphs, and Gantt charts. Allows step-by- step tracing of

uction timing charts, register allocation graphs, and Gantt charts. Allows step-by- step tracing of the compilation and execution stages. 6. Security and Usability Module Implements automated checks for unsafe code patterns, invalid instruction sequences, or register conflicts. It offers a user-friendly interface with export report capabilities. In a program in which several classes support collections of objects, this declaration should be in its own file. 3. Workflow methodology An eight-phase methodology to develop and evaluate the compiler backend emulator includes the following: 1. Requir

methodology to develop and evaluate the compiler backend emulator includes the following: 1. Requirement Analysis: Identify functional and non- functional requirements, supported architectures, and data flows. 2. System Design: Draw architectural diagrams and interaction models between the modules involved. Define data structures for instructions and registers. 3. Implementation: Use Python or C++ to code each of the emulator modules using object-oriented programming; implement scheduling algorithms such as list scheduling. 4. Integration Combine modules: emulator, visualization, metrics anal

ithms such as list scheduling. 4. Integration Combine modules: emulator, visualization, metrics analyzer, and validate their interoperability. 5. Testing and Validation: Compare the output of emulators with actual compiler backend outputs for accuracy and consistency. 7. Security & Usability Assessment: Perform code safety analysis, fault injection testing, and usability analysis of interfaces. 8. GANTTCHART VISUALIZATION METHODOLOGY As part of our methodology, Gantt-chart style visualizations are used to illustrate the timeline of backend tasks for a given compilation unit or block of code. These include stages such as instruction selection, scheduling, register allocation, code emission, optimization passes, and traversals through the backend pipeline. Concurrency, contention, stalls, and changing resource use over time are depicted in the Gantt chart. Here is a conceptual methodology: 1. Instrument the backend/emulator to t

picted in the Gantt chart. Here is a conceptual methodology: 1. Instrument the backend/emulator to timestamp each major phase/sub-phase of processing, such as time at the start/end of instruction selection, scheduling, allocation, etc. 2. For each basic block/function log the following events: IR ready for input, selection completed, scheduling initiated, register allocation initiated/completed, code emission completed 3. Create a Gantt chart where the horizontal axis is time (or normalized time units) and each row is a task or subtask. Examples include "Select instructions for block A", "Sche

units) and each row is a task or subtask. Examples include "Select instructions for block A", "Schedule block A" "Emit block A" 4. Overlay resource usage, including number of instructions in queue, number of live registers, and hazard events (pipeline stalls) on the chart. 5. Compare across runs - for example, different optimization levels or different target backends - by comparing Gantt charts side-by-side. A sample Gantt chart visualization: This methodology provides a clear way to visualize how the back-end/emulator handles blocks over time, when performance bottlenecks such as long sched

how the back-end/emulator handles blocks over time, when performance bottlenecks such as long scheduling stalls occur, and how changes in back-end strategy or target affect timing. Information is repeated to both the reader and the listener. VIII . PERFORMANCE METRICS CALCULATION We define formulas and empirical measurement procedures for our key performance metrics: Code size (CS): CS = number of bytes in generated machine code (or assembly) Runtime performance (RT): RT = \fr ac {\text {execution time on target hardware (or cycle count) }} (\text {baseline execution time} Compilation time (C

ion time on target hardware (or cycle count) }} (\text {baseline execution time} Compilation time (CT): CT = the wall-clock time taken for the backend code generation (instruction selection + scheduling + allocation + emission) SC: Spill count SC = \text{number of register-spills /reloads generated by backend} Instruction Count (IC): IC = number of target instructions produced by backend AP-Accuracy of prediction : AP = \\\\left|\\\\frac{RT_{emu) -RT_{real}}{RT_{real}} \\\\right| \\\\times 100\\\\% Usability score-US : a composite metric; for instance, time for developer to diagnose a backend

\\% Usability score-US : a composite metric; for instance, time for developer to diagnose a backend issue using a visualization tool, number of issues resolved, normalized. Measurement process: run each benchmark on target backends and emulator, measure metrics, calculate averages and standard deviations, as well as comparative ratios: e.g., CS (emu){real}{emu}{real). etc. Statistical tests (t-test) check significance of the differences. Once the execution is complete, the system calculates the following important parameters: Completion Time (CT): The instant when the execution of the process

following important parameters: Completion Time (CT): The instant when the execution of the process completes. Turnaround Time (TAT): The total time duration of the process in the system. Formula: TAT = CT − Arrival Time Waiting Time (WT): The total time a process spends waiting in the ready queue. Formula: WT = TAT − BT IX.SECURITY AND USABILITY FEATURES Sanity-checks of generated code, that the back-end/emulator code generates proper IL: e.g., no illegal register usages or aliasing, no buffer overflows in generated code sequences, no unintentional side-effects.
overflows in generated code sequences, no unintentional side-effects.

The emulator supports fault injection: e.g. simulate mis- scheduling, missed hazard detection, pipeline hazards to analyze how backend behaviours could lead to insecure or unpredictable generated code The suspicious patterns that this visualization tool flags are excessive spills, high register pressure, and unresolved dependencies, which may relate to vulnerabilities including timing side-channels and increased code size leading to instruction cache attacks. Emulation logs metadata, for each generated block, include the provenance of IR to machine instruction mapping, which allows auditing/tr

nerated block, include the provenance of IR to machine instruction mapping, which allows auditing/tracing of back-end decisions-usability/security audit trail. Usability Features Interactive visualization dashboard: Gantt charts, live-range graphs, pipeline view, instruction stream viewer. Ability to single-step over generated code one block at a time. View IR → machine mapping. Inspect schedules, hazards, resource usage. Comparative overlay mode: Switch seamlessly between two backends/emulator runs and see side-by-side differences. Export the metrics and logs in CSV or JSON format for externa

runs and see side-by-side differences. Export the metrics and logs in CSV or JSON format for external analysis. User annotations: Developers can mark backend phases; add comments and link visualization items to issues/bugs.User- friendly GUI and CLI for automation at the service of researchers and practitioners. These features have the goal of making the compiler backend workflows (diagnosis, tuning, retargeting) more usable and, by making the backend internals visible, auditable and analyzable, also more secure. X. RESULTS We have implemented the backend emulator framework and conducted exper

also more secure. X. RESULTS We have implemented the backend emulator framework and conducted experiments with a benchmark suite (e.g., MI Bench) on two target backends, an x86 production backend, and an embedded ASIP backend. Key results: Code size: On average, the code size generated by the emulator was 1.05x of the code generated on the real backend for x86 and 1.10x for ASIP. Runtime performance: Emulator predicted RT_{emu} within ±12% of RT_{real} for x86, ±18% for ASIP. Compilation time: It took on average Generate an emulator backend in a time proportional to ~0.8x the real backend tim

It took on average Generate an emulator backend in a time proportional to ~0.8x the real backend time, making for fast experimentation. Spill count and instruction count were highly correlated between emulator and real backend, Pearson r≈ 0.88. Usability: Developers working with Visualization tool reported that the median time to find the scheduling bottlenecks decreased by ~30%. Security: Two patterns of high The fault injection experiments found register-pressure plus long scheduling stall sequences that correlate with increased timing variability, flagged via a visual tool. uences that correlate with increased timing variability, flagged via a visual tool. In the following tables and graphs, which are not included in this summary, the metrics are broken down per benchmark, per backend, and per optimization level. XI. DISCUSSION These results indicate that the backend emulator makes reasonably accurate predictions both about code size and runtime, especially for the mainstream target (x86). The higher deviation of the ASIP backend is expected due to its less mature modelling of the target pipeline and hazard behaviour. Improved compilation time and increased usabi

modelling of the target pipeline and hazard behaviour. Improved compilation time and increased usability through visualization stand as strong advantages, supporting fast backend tuning and debugging. Security insights via fault-injection and pattern detection by means of visualization show new possibilities: exposing backend internals might allow to detect backend-induced vulnerabilities such as timing side-channels and register pressure leaks. However, limitations remain: The accuracy of the emulator depends on quality of the target pipeline/hazard model. For more complex or heterogeneous ar

mulator depends on quality of the target pipeline/hazard model. For more complex or heterogeneous architectures, like vector, SIMD, or tensor-accelerators, the modelling is much harder. Benchmarks used here have moderate size. The scalability issues may well happen for large-scale applications with regard to visualization/dashboard. Usability results in this paper are based on a small number of developers; more extensive user studies are needed. These security features are preliminary and would be further strengthened with additional, more systematic threat modeling and attack scenarios. Futur

be further strengthened with additional, more systematic threat modeling and attack scenarios. Future work could thus include: extension to tensor-accelerator backends, drawing on work such as ACT; integrating formal verification of backend correctness; expanding the visualization to distributed/heterogeneous compilation flows; and deeper usability or security studies.

XII.CONCLUSION Conclusion In this paper, we presented the architecture for a compiler-backend emulator with rich visualization, comparative metrics, usability, and security features. We overviewed related research, pointed out the gaps, detailed our Gantt-chart methodology, performance metrics, and then implemented the system with empirical evaluation. The results support the utility of the emulator for rapid experimentation, back-end tuning, and developer usability as well as providing early security insights. We hope this work opens further research into backend emulation, visualization of c

curity insights. We hope this work opens further research into backend emulation, visualization of compiler internals, and developer-centric tools for compiler backend engineering.
